text: The Stoic philosopher Hierocles proposed a series of concentric circles — self, family, community, city, nation, humankind — and urged us to 'draw the outer circles inward,' extending our moral concern outward while shrinking our egocentric perspective. Modern cognitive psychologists call a similar failure 'construal level theory': we think about distant things abstractly and nearby things concretely, which means local irritants feel more vivid and urgent than they deserve. Hierocles' exercise was a deliberate correction of this bias, training the mind to see the local through the lens of the universal before deciding how much attention to award it.
question: According to the passage, what cognitive bias does Hierocles' concentric circles exercise correct?

options.text: The tendency to prioritize future consequences over present discomfort
The construal level bias that makes nearby irritants feel more urgent than they deserve
The moral failure to extend concern beyond one's immediate family
Overconfidence in abstract reasoning at the expense of concrete experience
